content:
Hey! This was just a small thought of mine and I'm not too attached to it, but while I was writing some of the vblank bindings for KMS I noticed we actually have a few functions that rely on the caller holding a particular lock. At first I started acquiring the locks with normal unsafe calls to the C bindings but I realized it might make more sense if we added a ::from_raw() to Lock<(), B: Backend> (so, just lock types without directly associated data) so that rust bindings for C interfaces can get a rust-like reference to locks they may have that were initialized on the C side of things. Does this seem like something people would be interested in if I sent patches for it in the near future?
We would need to add a clause to the safety contract for Backend that the implementor use #[repr(C)] or something so that when T == () the layout of Lock<(), B> is equivalent to B::State but that seems easy
Seems reasonable enough to me.

Lyude said:

Hey! This was just a small thought of mine and I'm not too attached to it, but while I was writing some of the vblank bindings for KMS I noticed we actually have a few functions that rely on the caller holding a particular lock. At first I started acquiring the locks with normal unsafe calls to the C bindings but I realized it might make more sense if we added a ::from_raw() to Lock<(), B: Backend> (so, just lock types without directly associated data) so that rust bindings for C interfaces can get a rust-like reference to locks they may have that were initialized on the C side of things. Does this seem like something people would be interested in if I sent patches for it in the near future?

sounds reasonable.
Are the functions that you need to call while holding the lock C functions? I.e. do you have this pattern?:

pub struct Foo(Opaque<bindings::foo>);

impl Foo {
    pub fn bar(&self) -> u32 {
        let ptr = self.0.get();
        let bar_lock = unsafe { addr_of!((*ptr).bar_lock) };
        let bar_lock = unsafe { Lock::from_raw(bar_lock) };
        let _guard = bar_lock.lock();
        // SAFETY: we hold the bar_lock.
        unsafe { (*ptr).bar }
    }
}

We would need to add a clause to the safety contract for Backend that the implementor use #[repr(C)] or something so that when T == () the layout of Lock<(), B> is equivalent to B::State but that seems easy

I don't think that Backend has anything to do with that, just putting #[repr(C)] on Lock should be enough.

Benno Lossin said:

Lyude said:

Hey! This was just a small thought of mine and I'm not too attached to it, but while I was writing some of the vblank bindings for KMS I noticed we actually have a few functions that rely on the caller holding a particular lock. At first I started acquiring the locks with normal unsafe calls to the C bindings but I realized it might make more sense if we added a ::from_raw() to Lock<(), B: Backend> (so, just lock types without directly associated data) so that rust bindings for C interfaces can get a rust-like reference to locks they may have that were initialized on the C side of things. Does this seem like something people would be interested in if I sent patches for it in the near future?

sounds reasonable.
Are the functions that you need to call while holding the lock C functions? I.e. do you have this pattern?:

pub struct Foo(Opaque<bindings::foo>);

impl Foo {
    pub fn bar(&self) -> u32 {
        let ptr = self.0.get();
        let bar_lock = unsafe { addr_of!((*ptr).bar_lock) };
        let bar_lock = unsafe { Lock::from_raw(bar_lock) };
        let _guard = bar_lock.lock();
        // SAFETY: we hold the bar_lock.
        unsafe { (*ptr).bar }
    }
}

Yep - basically :), as well there's a few objects I have at the moment that themselves are either indicators that a lock is already held, or act as a guard for such and provide access to methods that can only happen under lock

Benno Lossin said:

I don't think that Backend has anything to do with that, just putting #[repr(C)] on Lock should be enough.

Ah yes you're right lol, whoops

Lyude said:

Yep - basically :), as well there's a few objects I have at the moment that themselves are either indicators that a lock is already held, or act as a guard for such and provide access to methods that can only happen under lock

hmm so it would make more sense to allow you to get a Lock<T, B> where T is a ZST?

Benno Lossin said:

Lyude said:

Yep - basically :), as well there's a few objects I have at the moment that themselves are either indicators that a lock is already held, or act as a guard for such and provide access to methods that can only happen under lock

hmm so it would make more sense to allow you to get a Lock<T, B> where T is a ZST?

It might sometimes but it depends. The lock I'm thinking of right now, drm_device.event_lock, I'm not really sure yet I've found any situations where we'd actually expose a guard for it to a user - only to ourselves within bindings. Most of the time that I've been exposing types that represent holding a lock, they usually aren't ZST and hold a reference to the struct in question so it can provide methods to call on it
@Benno Lossin I guess we could just allow any ZST T with a build_assert!() right?
yes
hm, well I can't see a reason to say no so sgtm

You can't just unconditionally allow all ZST. For example, ! is a ZST but allowing the creation of &mut ! would not be good. Similarly, if I make a token type where having an instance of it represents proof of some invariant, that may let you launder a token in cases where the invariant does not hold.
the function will still be unsafe
since it takes a raw pointer to begin with, so we can just add the invariant that you are allowed to create that ZST
but you are right it's something that we need to add/consider
from_raw() should return a Pin<&Lock<..>>, right?
Otherwise you can move the lock

Boqun Feng said:

from_raw() should return a Pin<&Lock<..>>, right?

I don't think so - you can't move data out from a immutable reference
oh, so it returns a &Lock<..>?
This is also why Pin<T> implements Deref and not DerefMut, &T technically withholds the safety requirements of pinning

Boqun Feng said:

oh, so it returns a &Lock<..>?

yes
That works as well.
Then should it have a different name? Googling...
from_raws ususally convert a raw pointer into a smart pointer (or an object of a type).

Boqun Feng said:

from_raws ususally convert a raw pointer into a smart pointer (or an object of a type).

yeah - hold on, I think I have the naming convention stuff in my history
https://rust-lang.github.io/api-guidelines/naming.html#ad-hoc-conversions-follow-as_-to_-into_-conventions-c-conv
hold on, that's only into...
into is just another direction of from.
yeah true
The problem is though "as" doesn't really have another form like "into" does with "from" though
(since otherwise, I think an as_ prefix would be more appropriate since it's just transmuting)
It wouldn't be borrow would it?
You're borrowing, since the lifetime of the &Lock cannot outlive the owner of the object.
Guess I'll have to convert a bunch of the KMS bindings over to borrow as well since we have quite a few functions like this. So ::borrow() then?
Maybe, but the problem with ::borrow() is that you are not using the &Lock to access anything, just for locking.
but once we have it (whatever name we give it), maybe we get more ideas about the name after some actual usage.

BTW - I am considering doing the same thing here for Guard<'a, (), B> where B: Backend<GuardState = ()> (and also exposing Guard as something that can be imported outside of the kernel crate). The main usecase is for privileged smart-pointers that allow access to certain methods/data protected by a given lock. At least in DRM, we have the drm_device.mode_config.mutex BFL that a number of random tidbits are protected by. And it's a lock that usually ends up both getting locked explicitly by drivers, and as well some DRM object callbacks occur with the lock already held (and require the driver to do something protected by said lock). Should I just make this part of the same series?

(Realizing that might be the way to go anyway, because both interfaces would rely on Lock<'a, (), B> having an identical data layout to B::State - so it's probably worth documenting this as an invariant of Lock anyway) nevermind, I don't think they'd actually be relying on this (directly anyway, due to the fact that we'd implement this through Lock<'a, (), B>::from_raw() anyway.
There is one nicety this would add though - going to make it so that from_raw and friends actually verify that the given lock is held when possible (and add something to Backend to enable this)
